title: UV-Sterilized Bioreactor — Biosphere
summary: Three years of R&D reinventing how a bioreactor stays sterile — using UV light instead of steam. I owned the mechanical design, materials, and reliability work behind the reactor and its impeller-seal system, then helped scale it from a bench prototype to a $9M DOD pilot.
detail: R&D on UV-based sterilization for bioreactors at Biosphere — mechanical design, materials and surface engineering, design of experiments, and scale-up from benchtop to pilot.

outcomes.label: Lower system cost than the existing industry-standard sterilizable reactor
Reliability testing logged on the impeller and seal system
Follow-on DOD pilot program the benchtop work was scaled into

sections.title: The Problem
sections.body: Bioreactors are normally sterilized with steam, which constrains how they're built and run. The program I worked on set out to reinvent that around UV light — which meant rethinking the mechanical design, the materials, and every internal surface so the whole vessel could be sterilized by light, with nothing left to harbor organisms. It was research-driven product design: developing new sterilization and manufacturing methods from first principles, not adapting an existing reactor.

sections.title: What I Owned
sections.body: Over roughly three years I took on multiple projects, all pointing at the same goal — a scalable, manufacturable system that stays sterile under UV and costs less than the existing industry standard. I owned the mechanical design of the reactor's functional features and led the impeller and seal subsystem end to end, ran the design-of-experiments behind the validation, owned the supply chain and project documentation, and did the modeling to scale the design up.

sections.title: Reactor & Sterile-Barrier Design
sections.body: The first benchtop reactor was a $1.5M DOD project, and I designed most of the functional features, ports, and subsystems that go into it — all specifically for UV. That meant mechanical fixtures that prevent organism buildup at the scale of microns, gasket designs with worked-out squeeze and tolerance calculations, and assembly sequences built for repeatability and high cycle counts. Extreme tolerance requirements drove the analysis of the right gasket squeeze, the right fit, and the right order of assembly.

sections.title: Impeller & Seal System
sections.body: The core of my work: a rotating impeller that holds full sterility and pressure while the shaft spins at 3,000 RPM, with liquid, heat constraints, and a complete barrier between the inside of the reactor and the outside world — all while staying UV-sterilizable and leak-free. I ran material testing on shaft and seal candidates, designed the fittings and housings, and reduced the number of contact areas to keep it reliable. I worked directly with Omni seals, lip-seal makers, and bearing suppliers, and improved impeller performance by ~35% while cutting heat generation ~20%. The system was validated over more than 10,000 hours of testing.

sections.title: Materials & Surface Engineering
sections.body: A lot of this came down to materials science. Surface finish drives both sterility and part life, so I worked through polishing methods and grades, vapor polishing, and electropolishing, and matched surface treatments to the job. I worked with plastics and specialty plastics, polymers and epoxies, laser cutting and welding, and ran the material analysis behind scoring prevention — stopping the surface degradation that would otherwise shorten the impeller's life. Life-cycle testing tied it all back to manufacturing reliability.

sections.title: UV Dosage Simulation
sections.body: Because the whole system is sterilized by light, any shadowed region is a sterility risk. I built ray-tracing simulations inside the CAD model to confirm every internal surface receives the right UV dose, and tested that all locations hit the threshold needed to sterilize the full vessel.

sections.title: Design of Experiments & Reliability
sections.body: A bioreactor is a multi-variable system — chemistry, humidity, pressure, biology, and acidity all moving at once — and finding what's going wrong is hard when you're starting from scratch. I broke the system into subsystems I could test with confidence, while keeping the combined test valid so results still held when the pieces came back together. I also increased the number of output assays per run to shorten a three-day test, designed the experiments to hold 95% confidence reliability, and worked through operator fatigue and step-count with the people running the tests — cutting validation time by 40–45%.

sections.title: Manufacturing & Supply Chain
sections.body: I had full ownership of the supply chain — sourcing, connecting suppliers, running meetings, and managing the logistics — and worked with manufacturers to find the cheapest, most scalable way to build each part. Cutting the number of manufacturing steps dropped cost ~35% and lead time 40–45%. Alongside it I wrote the project and experimental-assay documentation, planned the future scope of the work, and broke it down into tasks and schedules (Gantt) so the program stayed coordinated.

sections.title: Scale-Up
sections.body: The last step is going from a working benchtop system to a pilot. I translated the bench design into a pilot-scale design — handling form factor, modularity, safety, and cost so it could grow without losing what made it work — for a follow-on DOD program funded at $9M. I led the phase-one validation testing and the CAD modeling of how the form factor behaves at the larger scale while staying compact and modular.

gallery.caption: Lab scale developed for microbial oil for DoD

title: Antifoam Detection — Vision + ML
summary: Built a camera-and-ML system that watches inside a live bioreactor and releases antifoam at exactly the right moment — turning a failure that can scrap a run worth thousands to millions into a hands-off safety system.
detail: A vision + ML pipeline that detects foaming inside a running bioreactor and triggers antifoam dosing automatically — built end to end, from the hardware that captures the image to the model that reads it.

outcomes.label: Cost of a single foam-out — from a bench run to a full-scale reactor — that the system prevents
Runs autonomously at sites with no operator present, the constraint that made it necessary
Real images expanded through augmentation into enough labeled data to train on

sections.title: The Problem
sections.body: Foaming is one of the failure modes that can ruin an entire bioreactor run. On a small bench reactor that's roughly $2,500; at full scale it can be millions of dollars. For the DOD program these reactors had to run in locations with no operators on site, so foam control couldn't depend on someone watching — it had to be automatic, and it had to be right. It was one of the things that keeps an operations team up at night.

sections.title: Seeing Inside the Reactor
sections.body: The hard part is just getting a usable image inside a sealed, pressurized, sterile vessel. I worked through the options — putting a camera in the head plate behind a simple viewport versus building a glass vessel — and evaluated how the project had to be set up to get a clear, repeatable image without compromising sterility or pressure. Then I designed the hardware path to capture it.

sections.title: Building the Dataset
sections.body: I captured images across conditions, labeled them, and extracted the labels into a usable training set. With only a small number of real images, I leaned on augmentation to expand the data — turning on the order of 10 real data points into roughly 50 — so there was enough labeled data to actually train on.

sections.title: The Model & Pipeline
sections.body: I took a pre-trained image model and fine-tuned it for this specific detection task, then built the full pipeline around it: image capture, processing, inference, and the decision logic. The system reads each frame, recognizes when foam is forming, and triggers antifoam dosing at the right moment to keep the run alive — including tuning how much antifoam to release and validating how well it held.

sections.title: Why It Mattered
sections.body: Beyond keeping runs alive, this was the project that deepened how I integrate hardware and software: shaping the hardware so the software can see, using an existing ML model for the analysis, and driving a real physical safety action from its output. It's the clearest example of manipulating a hardware system to make software accessible — and using that to protect a process that's expensive to lose.

title: Omni-Directional Treadmill
summary: Led the product design team building a VR-integrated rehabilitation treadmill — concept to prototype, 44% smaller, presented at the Fung Institute Capstone Showcase.
detail: Objective: Build a compact, multi-directional treadmill using omni wheels and control logic that keeps users centered at all times, in collaboration with Blue Goji.

outcomes.label: Height reduction of the drive system across three months of iteration
Planar motion achieved by combining motor speed variations
Presented at the Fung Institute Capstone Showcase

sections.title: Project Objective
sections.body: Design a multi-directional treadmill using omni wheels so a user can move freely in any direction within a fixed space. The goal was to reduce the size of the existing prototype while adding a control system to keep the user centered. This was done with Blue Goji to improve physical and brain health.

sections.title: Principle
sections.body: A single-motor treadmill enables infinite motion in one direction. By combining two motors, motion can be produced in two axes. With the right motor speed variations, any 2D planar motion can be achieved.

sections.title: Approach
sections.body: We focused on downsizing without sacrificing efficiency or integrity. After multiple iterations over three months, we redesigned the drive system, reduced height by 44%, and increased safety factor using stress-concentrator analysis and topological optimization.

sections.title: Contribution
sections.body: I led the product design team, modified one drive system, and supported other sub-teams with FEA, rapid prototyping, proof-of-concept builds, and fabrication. I completed the full assembly in SOLIDWORKS, ran structural analysis on the platform, and contributed to fabrication, testing, and final assembly. The work culminated in a presentation at the Fung Institute Capstone Showcase.

sections.title: Note
sections.body: Specifics are withheld due to NDA restrictions.

title: Off-the-Shelf Parts Copilot
summary: An Onshape tool that reads your design as you model it and points you to off-the-shelf parts that already fit — cutting custom parts, cost, and prototyping lead time before you ever commit to a design.
detail: A design-time tool inside Onshape that recognizes what you're modeling and surfaces existing off-the-shelf parts that fit, so early prototypes lean on parts you can order in a day instead of custom-manufacturing them.

outcomes.label: Off-the-shelf where it fits means lower cost and no in-house manufacturing loop
Vendor delivery in place of a full design-make-test cycle for early prototypes

sections.title: The Problem
sections.body: Engineers often can't easily tell what already exists off the shelf, so they over-engineer or design parts from scratch. That drives cost up — you can rarely beat the price of a part that's already manufactured at scale — and it adds a full loop: design the part, model it, validate it, manufacture it (3D print, or send it out and wait on lead time), get it back, assemble, and test. For early prototyping, most of that loop is avoidable.

sections.title: The Idea
sections.body: A tool that lives inside Onshape and understands what you're modeling as you go. If you're designing a shaft running from a motor to a wheel, it recognizes the geometry in context and tells you there's an existing shaft close to that diameter you can drop in — surfacing the off-the-shelf options right at the moment you're making the decision, instead of after you've already designed a custom part.

sections.title: Why It Helps
sections.body: Off-the-shelf parts mean one or two day delivery from most vendors instead of an in-house or overseas manufacturing cycle. In the startup world especially, validating an early prototype with off-the-shelf parts is faster and cheaper — and the value is in giving that access exactly when it's needed, as the concept is being designed, rather than leaving it as something to chase down later.

sections.title: Where It's Going
sections.body: The next step is using rough sketches and diagrams — converting that imagery to estimate, before any detailed design starts, how much of a concept can be built from existing parts versus what genuinely needs to be custom. It's an ongoing side project.

title: Rumifico — Sharing Memories
summary: Took a human-centered product from user research to award-winning concept with HP and a Google UX designer — Best Overall at the Jacobs Showcase 2022.
detail: Human-centered design project with HP and a Google UX designer to reconnect parents and college students through shared memories.

outcomes.label: Award at the UC Berkeley College of Engineering Jacobs Showcase 2022
Built alongside HP mentors and a Google UI/UX designer

sections.title: Overview
sections.body: Rumifico is a nostalgic, 21st‑century pager designed to help parents and kids who have moved out for college stay connected through shared memories.

sections.title: Collaboration
sections.body: We worked alongside HP and a Google UI/UX designer, applying human‑centered design to turn a meaningful connection gap into a tangible product concept.

sections.title: Recognition
sections.body: The project won the Best Overall Award at the UC Berkeley College of Engineering Jacobs Showcase 2022.

sections.title: Project Link
sections.body: https://www.behance.net/gallery/159089991/RUMIFICO-SHARING-MEMORIES/modules/897669701

title: Oxygen Concentrator
summary: Co-led design of a low-cost PSA oxygen concentrator for COVID relief — concept to manufacturable product, solving real production failures with DFMA and original mechanisms.
detail: Mission: Address the COVID-19 oxygen shortage in India by delivering a low-cost, compact PSA oxygen concentrator for small to medium hospitals (5–8 beds). The goal was a miniature, manufacturable alternative to full-scale PSA plants.

outcomes.label: Compact PSA concentrator scaled down from a 225 LPM plant design
Spring plate fix eliminating zeolite wear, adopted across both projects
By community leaders including the Honorable central minister during the crisis

sections.title: Mission
sections.body: This project supported the Government of India’s effort to solve the medical-grade oxygen shortage during COVID-19. We targeted a low-cost, compact PSA concentrator (25 LPM) for small to mid-size hospitals, designed as a miniature counterpart to on-site PSA plants.

sections.title: Principle
sections.body: Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) uses synthetic zeolite sieves to adsorb nitrogen at high pressure, releasing oxygen. Two sieve beds alternate between pressurization and depressurization to maintain a continuous oxygen flow.

sections.title: Timeline of Work
sections.body: We began by modeling PSA valve timing and scaling from a 225 LPM plant to a 25 LPM concentrator. After simulations, we built a prototype to validate parameters while parallelizing enclosure and system layout design. Vendor manufacturing handled pressure vessels, allowing us to focus on assembly speed, cost, and serviceability.

sections.title: Design & Validation
sections.body: As one of two Product Design leads, I iterated the framework around constraints including size, weight, materials, aesthetics, electronics placement, mobility, airflow, and serviceability. Designs were reviewed with the engineering team and client and approved by the Technical Director.

sections.title: Key Contribution — Closure System
sections.body: To avoid welded thin sheet metal and exposed sharp edges, I developed a manufacturable closure system inspired by everyday mechanisms (CPU slide panels, angled screws in wood frames, and generator enclosures). This enabled safe, repeatable assembly for mass production.

sections.title: Key Contribution — Spring Plate Fix
sections.body: Testing revealed a small air gap at the top dish end plate that could grow over time as zeolite granules moved under cyclic pressure, increasing wear. After evaluating alternatives, I designed a compressed spring plate with seven springs to eliminate the gap as it formed. This prevented granule wear, improved efficiency, and was adopted in both projects.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body: The project was recognized by community leaders, including the Honorable central minister and regional hospitals, for its contribution during the crisis.

title: PSA Oxygen Plant
summary: Ran production as manager for an on-site hospital oxygen plant — led a 40+ person team across supply chain, fabrication, quality, and delivery under tight deadlines.
detail: Mission: Deliver large-scale hospitals an on-site PSA oxygen plant that provides continuous oxygen with minimal service needs and eliminates cylinder transport costs.

outcomes.label: Team members coordinated: machinists, welders, engineers, and support staff
Continuous oxygen generation, eliminating cylinder transport costs

sections.title: Mission
sections.body: Design and deploy a one-time setup, on-site PSA oxygen generation plant for large hospitals. The system delivers continuous oxygen with minimal service requirements, removing reliance on cylinders and lowering cost by eliminating transportation.

sections.title: My Role
sections.body: I worked as a Production Manager alongside the oxygen concentrator project. Responsibilities included supply chain coordination, vendor management, post‑fabrication partnerships, workforce oversight, and quality assurance.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.body: I coordinated a team of 18+ contract workers (machinists, assemblers, welders), 4 engineers, and 4 support staff. I ran board-level project meetings, visited vendors, and maintained timelines to meet tight client deadlines.

sections.title: Hands‑On Engineering
sections.body: I produced 2D fabrication drawings, developed sheet metal parts for laser cutting, and designed supports for pressure tanks. I also gained experience with post‑production processes including sandblasting, painting, pressure vessel testing, dye‑penetration tests, and material treatment.

title: Make Kit
summary: Built an educational maker service end to end — user research, concept generation, and three scalable product kits designed for real learning impact.
detail: Developed a maker kit program centered on the idea that 'Nothing teaches like a project.'

sections.title: Project Objective
sections.body: Develop an educational service for students, makers, and learners to gain new skills through hands‑on projects. The team researched, interviewed users, and generated concepts to create three scalable, reusable kits focused on usefulness and learning impact.

sections.title: Core Idea
sections.body: Nothing teaches like a project.

title: Cardiovascular FSI Research — SimVascular
summary: An ongoing line of open-source cardiovascular simulation research on SimVascular — one arterial FSI model plus two in-progress studies on patient hemodynamics and bypass-graft analysis, all aimed at making cardiovascular modeling accessible at far lower computational cost.
detail: Open-source fluid-structure interaction research using SimVascular — an arterial model on the coupled momentum method, and two follow-on studies toward publication on patient hemodynamics and CABG graft analysis.

outcomes.label: One arterial FSI model plus two in-progress studies toward publication
Comparable results to proprietary tools at significantly lower computational cost

sections.title: Overview
sections.body: This research promoted open-source tools for fluid-structure interaction (FSI) studies to reduce cost and computational barriers in cardiovascular care. I led the academic study at MIT Manipal, comparing standard proprietary workflows with open-source alternatives using a coronary artery model — and the work has since grown into a small line of cardiovascular simulation research on SimVascular.

sections.title: Ongoing Research
sections.body: Three studies sit on top of this work. The first is the arterial FSI model, using the coupled momentum method (CMM) together with image processing. The second, in progress, compares hemodynamics between a pulmonary-hypertension patient and a healthy patient — how the flow characteristics differ and what that tells us. The third analyzes CABG bypass grafts, comparing saphenous, mammary, and radial grafts to understand how each performs. The latter two are being prepared for publication.

sections.title: Tools
sections.body: We used SimVascular (SimTK), an open-source cardiovascular modeling and simulation suite developed at UC Berkeley and Stanford. Proprietary benchmarks used Mimics/CATIA for modeling and ANSYS Workbench for simulation prep.

sections.title: Method
sections.body: A coronary artery segment was modeled from MRI DICOM images with matched initial parameters across both workflows. We generated a 3D model, applied Delaunay tetrahedral meshing, and compared outcomes to evaluate the open-source pipeline’s effectiveness and improvement areas.

sections.title: Boundary Conditions
sections.body: Outlet conditions used an RCR Windkessel model, translating vascular behavior into an electrical analog (resistor, capacitor, current). Inlet waveforms and physiological values were sourced from literature.

sections.title: Roadblocks
sections.body: Two major issues were a lack of a system-wide units convention (CGS vs MKS) and limited Windows solver support. I wrote a Python scaling script for model.vtp files and configured an external solver to match SimVascular until platform support improved.

sections.title: Results
sections.body: Specific results are withheld as the work is being published. In comparison to standard tools, SimVascular provided comparable flow-parameter outputs with significantly lower computational cost and improved accessibility.

sections.title: Note
sections.body: This work is being published; detailed results are not disclosed.

title: Gravitational Effects on Boiling (ML)
summary: Studied gravity and Marangoni effects on boiling heat transfer using machine learning and genetic algorithms.
detail: Modeled water/2-propanol mixtures across varying gravitational conditions to identify optimized heat transfer behavior.

title: Solar PV Cell Optimization
summary: Built a neural network model to optimize PV cell organization for improved system performance.
detail: Validated the model with data and evaluated power output to identify best-performing cell combinations.

title: Boiler Performance Modeling
summary: Developed neural network models to predict boiler performance in solar thermal power plants.
detail: Trained models on real-world data for vertical tube boiling at 4.0 MPa and evaluated operating parameter impacts.

title: Turbine Blade Heat Transfer Analysis
summary: Analyzed heat transfer in turbine blades via modeling and experiments on an E423 airfoil.
detail: Investigated design optimization opportunities to improve thermal efficiency under varying operating conditions.

title: Engine Efficiency Through Thermal Design
summary: Designed a 6-cylinder radial pneumatic engine and improved efficiency through thermal-volume optimization.
detail: Modeled the engine in Autodesk Inventor and applied thermal mechanics to refine cylinder head geometry.
